items.correct: Colours opposite each other on the colour wheel that create strong contrast.
items.misconception: Colours that look similar and harmonise gently.
items.random_wrong: Colours used to flatter the artist.
items.why: Students confuse 'complementary' with 'matching nicely'. In colour theory, complementary colours (red/green, blue/orange, yellow/purple) intensify each other through CONTRAST, not similarity.

items.q: What did Renaissance artists rediscover that medieval art lacked?
items.correct: Linear perspective and naturalistic representation of the human body.
items.why: Students assume medieval art was colourless. It was vivid but flat - the Renaissance breakthrough was mathematical PERSPECTIVE (Brunelleschi) and anatomical realism, not the introduction of colour.

items.correct: Removing material from a solid block to reveal the form (e.g., carving stone).
items.why: Students confuse 'subtractive' with arithmetic. In sculpture, additive = building up (clay, welding); subtractive = taking away (carving). Michelangelo described 'freeing' figures from the marble - quintessential subtractive thinking.

items.q: Which contemporary British ceramicist is known for the 'Vase of Tears' and Turner Prize win in 2003?
items.why: Correct answer: Grayson Perry. “Edmund de Waal” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which discipline focuses on shaping the inside of buildings?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Interior design. “Architecture” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Grayson Perry win the Turner Prize?
items.why: Exact answer: 2003. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
